video_title: MacroVoices #536 Larry Mcdonald: The Migration is Upon us
gist: Larry McDonald argues markets are entering a 2021-22-style inflation-shock regime that triggers a 'great migration' out of crowded growth/tech and bonds into hard assets, value, energy, materials, healthcare and uranium. He frames a record IPO/insider-selling wave (SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) plus ~$3T of lockup overhang as a structural drain on the MAG7 and S&P, while Erik Townsend and Patrick Ceresna add that the Hormuz oil crisis is being suppressed by Trump jawboning, setting up a violent eventual oil spike.

insights.claim: Larry McDonald argues markets are repeating Q4 2021, when a 'transitory inflation' trance broke and equities fell roughly 35-40% with the NASDAQ shedding ~$7 trillion of value into 2022.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Implies a sizeable drawdown is likely as the market re-prices sticky inflation; position defensively in tech-heavy indices.
insights.quote: it's it's definitely a fourth quarter 2021 redux where everybody's kind of been in a transitory trance.

insights.claim: Super-core CPI is running at 3.7% and annualizing the last three months implies ~5.2% super-core by year-end, pointing to headline/core inflation of 6-8% a year out, per McDonald.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: If super-core (hard to fake) keeps accelerating, the disinflation trade unwinds; favors hard assets over duration and growth.
insights.quote: If you annualize the last 3 months of the super core inflation that came out, you're coming out to 5.2% by year end of super core inflation.

insights.claim: McDonald frames a structural shift from a unipolar disinflation decade to a multipolar world of global conflicts, higher rates and higher inflation, forcing a 'great migration' from financial assets (bonds, stocks, tech) into hard assets and the companies that control them.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: A reusable regime lens: rebuild portfolios around hard-asset owners and value rather than paper certificates.
insights.quote: that's what's really what we call in the book the great migration is coming at us.

insights.claim: Triple-C (tertiary high-yield) bond yields are blowing out even while stocks are near all-time highs, a leading-indicator divergence McDonald reads as a warning on the consumer.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Watch CCC spreads as an early tell that equity highs are unsupported by credit; risk-off catalyst.
insights.quote: The tertiary part of the high yield bond market which is triple C's as you could see they're really kind of blowing out.

insights.claim: McDonald contends the Fed cannot meaningfully hike despite an inflation shock because interest on the federal debt is now ~$1.1 trillion over the next 12 months versus ~$300 billion when the 2021-22 hiking cycle began.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: If 'Fed will hike' is a mirage, the curve should steepen sharply and front-end-driven pressure on gold reverses.
insights.quote: Never forget interest on the debt today is at two 1.1 trillion over the next 12 months... when the last time they started the hiking cycle in 21-22 it was 300 billion

themes.theme: Mega-IPO supply, insider selling and the overhang

insights.claim: The SpaceX IPO would equal ~6% of US GDP versus Facebook's 2012 IPO at under 1%, and combined with Google's ~$80B secondary implies ~$150B of immediate equity that must be funded by selling other stocks.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Mechanical capital drain pressures crowded large-caps; expect MAG7/S&P underperformance into the deals.
insights.quote: you're talking about an IPO that's 6% of US GDP... The SpaceX IPO would be 6% of GDP.

insights.claim: SpaceX, OpenAI, Anthropic and Google together imply roughly $200-250 billion of immediate equity raises in the next ~6 months, with SpaceX alone an ~$80B raise on a ~$1.8-2 trillion valuation.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Quantifies the near-term supply glut competing for capital allocation against the rest of the market.
insights.quote: There's about 200 250 billion of immediate raise.

insights.claim: Erik Townsend warns the bigger risk is the overhang: roughly $3 trillion of restricted insider/VC shares unlock 6-12 months after these IPOs (SpaceX lockup expiring around late 2026/end of 2027), far exceeding the direct raises.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: The 2027 lockup wall is the real bear case; watch unlock schedules as a forced-selling catalyst.
insights.quote: It's like 3 trillion of capital that gets unlocked as those uh restricted shares become unrestricted somewhere between 6 and 12 months after the IPO.

insights.claim: McDonald notes the SpaceX lockup/unlock schedule is more aggressive than prior IPOs and likens it to Facebook 2012, which suffered a 40-50% drawdown in its first year as lockups expired.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Suggests waiting to buy SpaceX 'roughly 50% off' within the first year rather than chasing the IPO.
insights.quote: you're going to probably be able to buy SpaceX IPO 50% off sometime in the first year.

insights.claim: Companies are now going public far later in their maturity cycle (Tesla IPO'd at ~$2B, Facebook ~$100B, SpaceX ~$2T), so most upside is captured privately and IPO buyers are 'bag holders' echoing 1999-2000.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Structural reason to fade late-stage mega-IPOs; the venture insiders, not public buyers, are the smart money exiting.
insights.quote: these companies are coming public much far later in the in the maturity cycle which means that the IPOs are very very unattractive

insights.claim: Convertible-bond issuance has spiked sharply over the last 3-4 weeks, which McDonald reads as CFOs (the 'smartest sellers') quietly selling embedded equity, mirroring the Q3-Q4 2021 surge that preceded the 2022 drawdown.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Rising convert supply is a stealth distribution signal; treat it as a top-warning indicator.
insights.quote: the smartest sellers in the world are chief financial officers. And guess what, Eric? The amount of convertible bonds that have been coming to the market... is up a lot over last year.

insights.claim: The S&P 500 is underperforming its equal-weight version over the last 10 days because MAG7 names (flat-to-negative since October) are the most liquid and easiest to sell to raise cash for IPOs.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Cap-weighted vs equal-weight divergence flags large-cap liquidation; favor breadth/equal-weight exposure.
insights.quote: huge underperformance in the last 10 days of the S&P versus the equal weight... people are selling the MAG 7

insights.claim: McDonald argues passive indexing near 60-65% of the market makes indices 'gameable,' letting West Coast VCs dump late-stage IPOs (e.g. SpaceX from ~$30B in 2019 to ~$1.8T) onto passive 'bag holders.'
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Implies systematic risk in passive flows and an edge for active managers as index inclusion is front-run.
insights.quote: passive investors are the bag holders up against really billionaire investors that have been in these IPOs for a decade

themes.theme: NASDAQ valuation blowoff and the rotation to value

insights.claim: The NASDAQ 100 ballooned from ~$30 trillion in late March to ~$41 trillion in under 50 trading days, a move McDonald calls unprecedented and a setup for a 2021-22-style decline (when it fell from ~$19T to ~$12T in four quarters).
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Parabolic market-cap expansion frames the magnitude of potential downside in mega-cap tech.
insights.quote: the NASDAQ 100 went from 30 trillion of value to 41 trillion in less than 50 trading days. Nothing like that's ever happened in the history of markets.

insights.claim: McDonald sees a bifurcated market: NASDAQ 100 at near-record CAPE/PE while energy, natural gas and materials trade at some of the cheapest free-cash-flow yields available.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Valuation dispersion is the trade: rotate from expensive tech into cheap hard-asset cash generators.
insights.quote: One part of the market, technology is really at the highest valuations almost ever. And then but in the energy space and in the material space, you've got beautiful free cash flow yields

insights.claim: McDonald expects a repeat of 2022's rotation winners, when energy stocks rose well over 100% and materials outperformed as money fled financial assets.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Directs capital toward energy and materials as the leadership of an inflation-shock regime.
insights.quote: If you look back to 2022 what were the big winners? Energy stocks were up a lot. I think well over 100%.

insights.claim: Growth-versus-value has failed lower for the first time since 2019, and McDonald calls the past week's value move the start of a 'colossal' rotation led by hard-asset owners like Berkshire Hathaway and Occidental.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Early signal to overweight value/hard-asset names ahead of a multi-year regime shift.
insights.quote: I think this is the beginning of a colossal move over toward value

insights.claim: McDonald expects a migration from the tech-heavy S&P 500 toward the Russell 2000, citing a wedge breakdown and the capital drain from IPO/bond supply.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Small-caps as a relative-value play if large-cap liquidation continues.
insights.quote: you'll see a great migration out of the S&P back over toward the Russell 2000.

insights.claim: The bottom 60-65% of consumers now account for only ~10-15% of consumption while the wealthy sit on high-yielding money-market funds, producing divergences like sharply weak restaurant stocks.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: K-shaped consumption argues for caution on discretionary names tied to the low-end consumer.
insights.quote: the bottom 65% of consumers are really only 10 15% of consumption now because the wealthy have so much money in money market funds

insights.claim: Consumer-discretionary names are deeply distressed: Home Depot is nearly 30% off and Lowe's, McDonald's and Harley-Davidson are roughly 19-20% off, with ~50% of Home Depot suppliers down 20-40%.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Breadth of discretionary weakness signals falling growth expectations beneath index highs.
insights.quote: Home Depot almost 30% off... there's probably 50% of Home Depot suppliers are down 20 30 40%.

themes.theme: Gold flush and the rates headwind

insights.claim: Gold sold off into the Iran conflict instead of rallying because rate-cut expectations flipped from three cuts to a possible one hike, and rising front-end T-bill yields (1-year up ~40bps) pull money out of non-yielding gold.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Explains the broken geopolitics-gold correlation; the front-end is the variable to watch for a gold bottom.
insights.quote: when front-end yields on T bills go up a lot it sucks money out of gold

insights.claim: McDonald uses a 'hot money flush' framework: the best trades follow a capitulation of weak-hand 'tourists,' which he says has now hit gold miners after they piled in during late 2024 and Q1, compounded by EM central banks selling gold to raise cash amid the energy shock.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Reusable capitulation lens; the flush creates the entry, scale in rather than pick a bottom.
insights.quote: the best trades of our careers are what I call the hot money flush.

insights.claim: McDonald flags Agnico Eagle (AEM) as a buy at ~40% off, ~5.9x EV/EBITDA (among its cheapest in 20-30 years), with $6-7B free cash flow and ~$2B buybacks, projecting gold at ~$6,500/oz next year and ~100% upside in the stock.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Concrete asymmetric idea (~10-15% downside vs ~200% up) for a slow-growth, high-inflation outcome.
insights.quote: your riskreward of buying AGO here is probably 10 15% down and 200% up

insights.claim: McDonald is buying gold miners in only thirds and quarters because Hormuz remains unresolved and miners could still fall another 50%; 'only monkeys pick bottoms.'
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Risk-management discipline: scale into a structurally cheap sector rather than going all-in early.
insights.quote: only monkeys pick bottoms, right?

insights.claim: Townsend warns gold may still be too early and sees room toward $3,900, then $3,400, and possibly a $3,000 test if the Hormuz crisis extends to year-end, given higher rates and a rising dollar as headwinds.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Sets downside technical targets for timing accumulation; the gold buying opportunity may come later in H2.
insights.quote: I don't think 3,000 is out of the question for the gold market to test that 30,000 round number support uh by by the end of the year.

themes.theme: Curve steepener and the rates trade

insights.claim: McDonald likes the 2s30s steepener (long 2-year, short 30-year), arguing the recent curve flattening is a 'mirage' because the Fed can't hike and the curve will steepen a lot over the next year; one vehicle is the IVOL ETF founded by Nancy Davis.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Actionable rates trade tied to the 'Fed can't hike' thesis; IVOL as a battered, contrarian expression.
insights.quote: so you like the 230 steepener long the 2-year and short the 30-year >> yes and one way to play that the Ival ETF

insights.claim: Townsend stays neutral on the 10-year, noting it is consolidating between ~4.45% and 4.65% with yields tracking oil-driven inflation expectations; he respects the intact uptrend in yields and thinks it is premature to call a yield peak.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Bond bull case depends on an oil/inflation peak that hasn't arrived; wait before buying duration.
insights.quote: I think it's a very premature to be looking for a peak in these yields

themes.theme: Inflation from AI capex, deficits and Hormuz

insights.claim: McDonald cites ~$2 trillion of data-center spending already plus a Street expectation of $5.4 trillion over the next 4-5 years (up from ~$3.5T eighteen months ago), stacked on ~$1.9T federal deficits and a 100-day Hormuz closure, as forces pushing inflation back to 5-6%.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Triangulates multiple structural inflation drivers; reinforces the hard-asset migration thesis.
insights.quote: another 5.4 4 trillion over the next four or five years. That's what the street's expecting.

insights.claim: McDonald argues oil-services names like SLB (Schlumberger) and Weatherford are overlooked AI beneficiaries that control valuable assets, and have already been outperforming the S&P this year.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: An off-consensus way to play AI without paying chip valuations.
insights.quote: The artificial intelligence potential of slumberge or SLB is literally one of the most exciting trades or investments I can think of

insights.claim: Healthcare has fallen from ~16% to ~8% of the S&P 500 even as boomers (avg ~70, controlling ~$79T) age, partly because managers and quant momentum players sell healthcare/staples to fund tech; McDonald calls it a colossal 5-year opportunity.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Deeply underowned sector with demographic tailwinds; candidate for the value/low-momentum rotation.
insights.quote: health care as a percentage of the S&P 500 has gone from 16% to 8%.

insights.claim: McDonald says the momentum factor (long semiconductors, short healthcare/staples) is at rare, stretched extremes, giving high probability of a quarter/month-end reversal out of high-momentum into low-momentum names in H2.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Factor crowding is the catalyst; fade momentum and rotate to healthcare for a potential H2 mean reversion.
insights.quote: the probability that we have a huge turn here I think is a very high probability of a move out of high momentum into low momentum

insights.claim: McDonald flags Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) as a screaming buy near its 200-week moving average, arguing its proprietary surgical-robotics data is an under-appreciated AI moat akin to Tesla's road data, per AI-medical family offices.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: A specific data-moat AI play inside the cheap, unloved healthcare complex.
insights.quote: if you buy intuitive surgical now on the 200E moving average to us that's a really screaming buy

themes.theme: Trapped gas and natural gas as the next AI play

insights.claim: McDonald pitches Tourmaline Oil as a 'trapped gas' AI play: with 800-1,000 data centers and ~$5T of spend expected over five years and NIMBY pushback siting some poorly, cheap stranded Canadian/Texas gas can power on-site turbines for hyperscalers (Tourmaline is reportedly in talks), with ~15-20% downside vs ~200% upside.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Links stranded gas to AI power demand; a high-asymmetry energy-AI idea.
insights.quote: you're taking trap gas and you're making it available to data centers and termine's in discussion with hyperscalers right now.

insights.claim: McDonald argues the Iran war and Hormuz closure raise the value of US and Canadian LNG/natural-gas assets as global LNG buyers seek supply away from the Middle East, a possibly unintended benefit of US action against Iran.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Geopolitical re-rating of North American gas exporters; bullish for LNG export-levered names.
insights.quote: what they've actually done is they've increased the value of US and Canadian gas assets.

themes.theme: Uranium: commodity now, miners after the flush

insights.claim: McDonald advises owning the uranium commodity (e.g. Sprott Physical Uranium Trust, SRUF) now and rotating into high-beta miners (URNM, NUKZ) only after a market washout, since miners fell 30-45% in the 2024 trade-war and 2024 yen-carry blow-ups.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Sequencing trade: use the commodity to ride volatility, then buy the miners into capitulation.
insights.quote: you want to be in the commodity if you expect volatility ahead and then you want to rotate into the miners during that big kind of huge draw down

insights.claim: McDonald cites a 2027-28 uranium supply-demand deficit worsened by producers (NexGen, Cameco, Denison) likely overstating production timelines by a year or two and a 'brain drain' of engineers into Bitcoin mining and AI during the long bear market.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: Supply slippage plus rising demand underpins the multi-year bull case; widening deficit is the catalyst.
insights.quote: they tend to overpromise on production... they're probably exaggerating by a year or two.

insights.claim: McDonald argues utility contract buyers, conditioned by the long bear market to sit on their hands, will be forced to ramp contracting over the next 12-18 months because uranium has no spot/futures market and they see the supply problem.
insights.speaker: Larry McDonald
insights.so_what: A wave of contract buying is the demand-side trigger to watch for a uranium re-rating.
insights.quote: these contract buyers are really going to have to step up the next 12 to 18 months because they see the supply and demand problem.

insights.claim: Townsend stays long-term bullish on uranium but warns a popping AI bubble or margin-call cascade could wash out the high-retail miners on the scale of late-2024-to-April-2025, and he is in no rush to add to buy-and-hold positions.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Reinforces buying the commodity now and waiting for a deeper miner drawdown before adding.
insights.quote: a popping of the AI bubble and a broad bare market in in stocks could easily mean a wash out for uranium

themes.theme: Hormuz oil suppression and the violent-spike setup

insights.claim: Townsend argues Trump's repeated peace-deal jawboning (CNN counted ~38-39 times since Feb 28) has scared speculators out of crude, keeping WTI flat-to-down despite a 100-day Hormuz closure and kinetic escalation.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Explains the disconnect between oil fundamentals and price; jawboning is the suppressing variable.
insights.quote: President Trump deserves an Oscar for his ability to jawbone the oil market down

insights.claim: Townsend predicts that once physical storage buffers exhaust and the futures market must rebalance, speculators scared off by Truth Social posts will pile in at once, producing a far more sudden and violent oil price spike than a normal front-run would.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Sets up an asymmetric long-oil thesis: suppressed positioning means an explosive move when physical forces a rebalance.
insights.quote: fear of missing out on the biggest price spike of all time... much more sudden and much more violent

insights.claim: Over 75% of sentiment-survey participants now expect lower oil prices soon; Townsend believes the consensus is wrong because the nuclear-file disagreement makes a near-term peace deal irreconcilable.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Crowded bearish positioning is the fuel for a violent repricing if no deal materializes.
insights.quote: sentiment surveys say that over 75% of market participants now expect lower prices and soon.

themes.theme: Equity technicals, dollar and systematic selling

insights.claim: Ceresna identifies 7,300 on the S&P 500 (the 50-day MA) as the key line: holding it through the SpaceX IPO could let the market rally back to highs, but a break risks ~$100-150B of CTA systematic selling and a slide toward 7,000 (~10% peak-to-trough).
insights.speaker: Patrick Ceresna
insights.so_what: A concrete trigger level: monitor 7,300 to gauge whether systematic flows accelerate a selloff.
insights.quote: many estimate that uh that selling kicks in around 7,300 on the S&P.

insights.claim: Townsend expects the dollar (DXY) to push toward 101.5-103 as the Iran conflict escalates, then top out and 'roll over hard' only once the conflict truly winds down.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: A dollar breakout above 52-week highs would ripple through cross-asset pricing; watch 100-103 resistance.
insights.quote: I think there's more upside at least to 101 and a half on the Dixie.

insights.claim: Townsend lays out a 2027 bear scenario where ~$3T of insider lockups expire alongside political turmoil (possible Democratic sweep, impeachments, gridlock) and an unresolved Iran conflict, risking mass insider exits and market panic.
insights.speaker: Erik Townsend
insights.so_what: Frames 2027 as the convergence risk window for equities; a multi-factor structural bear case.
insights.quote: There's going to be $3 trillion of equity that's being unlocked that people will be tempted... to get the heck out of and cash in
